title: Virtual environments and dependencies

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Virtual environments and dependencies; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Python Packaging User Guide; accessed 2026-07; Virtual Environments, Writing pyproject.toml, Packaging Projects, and Command-Line Tools guides; atomic topic: Virtual environments and dependencies
sources.claim: A Python virtual environment selects an isolated interpreter prefix and site-packages location so project dependencies do not modify the base interpreter's package set.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Python virtual environment selects an isolated interpreter prefix and site-packages location so project dependencies do not modify the base interpreter's package set.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The learner creates .venv, invokes that environment's python -m pip, records requirements, and confirms sys.prefix differs from sys.base_prefix. Your goal is to reproduce a project environment from documented interpreter and dependency requirements. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Function contracts and returns; Interpreter and environment; Packages and public APIs. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Python virtual environment selects an isolated interpreter prefix and site-packages location so project dependencies do not modify the base interpreter's package set. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Activation only adjusts shell command lookup, the environment is not generally portable by copying, system packages can be exposed by option, and dependency declarations still need versions and platform context. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Activating a virtual environment changes Python globally and guarantees every editor, task, and subprocess uses it.

includes: Create isolated environments and install only declared dependencies.
Reproduce a project environment from documented interpreter and dependency requirements.
excludes: Activation only adjusts shell command lookup, the environment is not generally portable by copying, system packages can be exposed by option, and dependency declarations still need versions and platform context.

outcomes: Reproduce a project environment from documented interpreter and dependency requirements.

examples.title: Virtual environments and dependencies: direct language case
examples.situation: The learner creates .venv, invokes that environment's python -m pip, records requirements, and confirms sys.prefix differs from sys.base_prefix.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: The learner creates .venv, invokes that environment's python -m pip, records requirements, and confirms sys.prefix differs from sys.base_prefix.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: A virtual environment is selected by its interpreter path; activation is a shell convenience, so verify the executable used by every tool.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because A virtual environment is selected by its interpreter path; activation is a shell convenience, so verify the executable used by every tool.

examples.title: Virtual environments and dependencies: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: An editor is configured to the same .venv interpreter as the terminal after imports differ despite an activated shell.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: An editor is configured to the same .venv interpreter as the terminal after imports differ despite an activated shell.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Activation only adjusts shell command lookup, the environment is not generally portable by copying, system packages can be exposed by option, and dependency declarations still need versions and platform context.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Activating a virtual environment changes Python globally and guarantees every editor, task, and subprocess uses it.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Reproduce a project environment from documented interpreter and dependency requirements.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Activating a virtual environment changes Python globally and guarantees every editor, task, and subprocess uses it.
misconceptions.correction: A virtual environment is selected by its interpreter path; activation is a shell convenience, so verify the executable used by every tool.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Activating a virtual environment changes Python globally and guarantees every editor, task, and subprocess uses it.

analogy.example: a library whose catalogs, shelves, isolated rooms, and shipping manifest separate import names from installable distributions can introduce Virtual environments and dependencies, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: imports, packages, virtual environments, and pyproject builds
limitations: sys.path, partial module initialization, build backends, editable installs, and wheel contents need direct inspection

recap.summary: You should now be able to reproduce a project environment from documented interpreter and dependency requirements. Keep this boundary in view: Activation only adjusts shell command lookup, the environment is not generally portable by copying, system packages can be exposed by option, and dependency declarations still need versions and platform context.
